passages.explanation: Jesus sees a man who has been blind since birth. His disciples ask whether the man's blindness is the result of his own sin or his parents' sin — reflecting a common assumption at the time that disability was punishment. Jesus rejects both options: the blindness exists so that God's work can be displayed through him. He repeats that he is the light of the world, then makes mud with his saliva, puts it on the man's eyes, and tells him to go wash in the pool of Siloam. The man does and comes back able to see.

The man's neighbours are confused. Some recognise him as the beggar who was blind; others think it's someone who looks like him. He confirms his identity. They ask how he can see. He explains exactly what happened — a man called Jesus made mud, put it on his eyes, and told him to wash. When they ask where Jesus is, he doesn't know.

The man is brought to the Pharisees. John notes again that this healing happened on a Sabbath. The Pharisees ask how he received his sight. He gives the same straightforward account. They split into two camps: some say Jesus can't be from God because he doesn't keep the Sabbath; others say a sinner couldn't perform such signs. When they ask the man his own opinion, he says Jesus is a prophet.

The Jewish authorities refuse to believe the man was actually born blind, so they summon his parents. The parents confirm he's their son and was born blind, but they refuse to explain how he can now see — they tell the authorities to ask him directly. John explains their caution: the authorities had agreed that anyone who acknowledged Jesus as the Christ would be expelled from the synagogue. The parents are protecting themselves.

The authorities call the man back a second time and tell him to give glory to God — essentially demanding he denounce Jesus as a sinner. His response is direct: whether Jesus is a sinner he doesn't know, but he knows one thing — he was blind and now he sees. They ask him again what Jesus did. He pushes back: he already told them and they didn't listen. Why do they want to hear it again — do they want to become Jesus' disciples too? The man has gone from cautious to confrontational.

The Pharisees insult him and claim to be Moses' disciples. They know God spoke to Moses, but they don't know where Jesus comes from. The man responds with plain logic: it's remarkable that they don't know where Jesus comes from, yet he opened a blind man's eyes. God doesn't listen to sinners but does listen to those who worship him and do his will. No one in history has ever given sight to someone born blind. If Jesus weren't from God, he couldn't do anything. The Pharisees are furious — they tell him he was born entirely in sin and has no right to teach them. They throw him out.

Jesus hears the man has been expelled and finds him. He asks if the man believes in the Son of God. The man asks who that is so he can believe. Jesus tells him: you've seen him, and he's the one talking to you right now. The man says 'Lord, I believe' and worships him. His journey through the chapter — from blind beggar to someone willing to defy the religious authorities to worshipping Jesus — is complete.

Jesus says he came into the world for judgment: so that the blind can see and those who think they see become blind. The Pharisees standing nearby ask if he's calling them blind. Jesus responds: if you were blind — if you genuinely didn't know — you'd have no guilt. But because you claim to see, your sin remains. Their problem isn't ignorance; it's that they're confident they already have the answers and refuse to recognise what's in front of them.
